perturbation.original_text: 1.3 "Affiliate" means, with respect to a particular Party, a Person that controls, is controlled by or is under common control with such Party. For the purposes of this definition, the word "control" (including, with correlative meaning, the terms "controlled by" or "under the common control with") means the actual power, either directly or indirectly through one (1) or more intermediaries, to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of such entity, whether by the ownership of fifty percent (50%) or more of the voting stock of such entity, by contract or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 "Affiliate" means, with respect to a particular Party, a Person that controls, is controlled by or is under common control with such Party. For the purposes of this definition, the word "control" (including, with correlative meaning, the terms "controlled by" or "under the common control with") means the advisory power, either directly or indirectly through recommendations, to influence the management and policies of such entity, irrespective of stock ownership or contractual obligations.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text redefines 'control' from actual power to 'advisory power' to influence management, removing the condition of stock ownership or contractual obligations. This contradicts the established legal and business understanding of 'control,' which typically requires a tangible means of directing management, such as majority ownership or a binding agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The SEC defines "control" as 'possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.' The modified contract language removes the requirement for 'power' and replaces it with 'advisory power,' and specifically states that stock ownership is irrelevant. This modification would be considered to not be a form of ‘control’ under securities regulations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o

perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'control' from 'actual power' to 'advisory power', specifically removing the requirement of stock ownership or contractual obligations. This contradicts the established legal understanding of 'control' because control typically requires a tangible means of directing management, such as majority ownership or a binding agreement. The SEC defines 'control' as 'possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.' The modified contract language removes the requirement for 'power' and replaces it with 'advisory power,' and specifically states that stock ownership is irrelevant. This modification would be considered to not be a form of ‘control’ under securities regulations. Although neither scraped snippet defines 'control' explicitly, they allude to the regulatory environment where this definition matters, thus supporting the argument that the change creates a legally significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.96 "Serious Adverse Event" means any serious untoward medical occurrence in a patient or subject who is administered a Licensed Product, having reference to the provisions of 21 C.F.R 312.32(a), but only if and to the extent that such serious untoward medical occurrence is required under Applicable Laws to be reported to applicable Regulatory Authorities.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.96 "Serious Adverse Event" means any untoward medical occurrence in a patient or subject who is administered a Licensed Product, regardless of severity or whether it is required to be reported to Regulatory Authorities.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'serious' qualifier and the condition that it must be reported under Applicable Laws, this modification broadens the definition of a 'Serious Adverse Event' to include any untoward medical occurrence, which can create ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 312.32(a) defines the scope and reporting requirements for adverse events, specifically addressing 'serious' and 'unexpected' adverse events. The modified definition removes the 'serious' qualifier, meaning any medical occurrence regardless of its nature must be reported. By not limiting the definition to occurrences that are 'serious', the contract imposes a broader and undefined reporting standard that clashes with the FDA regulation.

perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract broadens the definition of a 'Serious Adverse Event' by removing the 'serious' qualifier and the condition that it must be reported under Applicable Laws. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both highlight that this creates a contradiction with 21 CFR § 312.32(a), which specifies reporting requirements for 'serious' adverse events. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 21 CFR 312.32, indicating its relevance. By omitting the 'serious' qualifier, the contract imposes a reporting standard that clashes with FDA regulation, as any untoward medical occurrence regardless of its nature would need to be reported according to the changed text. This constitutes misaligned terminology creating a legal contradiction, as the contractual language deviates from the legally recognized definition of 'Serious Adverse Event'.

perturbation.original_text: 1.105 "Third Party" means any Person other than (a) Vyera, (b) CytoDyn or (c) an Affiliate of either of Vyera or CytoDyn.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.105 "Third Party" means any Person other than (a) Vyera, (b) CytoDyn or (c) an individual investor holding less than 1% equity in either of Vyera or CytoDyn.
perturbation.explanation: This modified definition significantly narrows the scope of 'Third Party' by excluding entities that are 'Affiliates.' The addition of the individual investor clause excludes entities based on investment size rather than affiliation, creating conflict.
perturbation.law_explanation: The SEC defines Affiliate in 17 CFR § 230.405 as 'a person that directly, or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, controls, or is controlled by, or is under common control with, the person specified.' The inclusion of ‘an individual investor holding less than 1% equity’ explicitly excludes some entities who would typically be considered Affiliates because they are a ‘person’ and not otherwise excluded. This undermines provisions that may protect each party against third party influence.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 230.405 - Definitions of terms. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 230.405 - Definitions of terms. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 230.405 Definitions of terms. Unless the context otherwise requires, all terms used in §§ 230.400 to 230.494, inclusive, or in the forms for registration have the same meanings as in the Act and in the general rules and regulations . In addition, the following definitions apply, unless the context otherwise requires: Affiliate. An affiliate of, or person affiliated with, a specified person, is a person that directly, or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, controls or is controlled by, or is under common control with, the person specified. Amount. The term amount, when used in regard to securities, means the principal amount if relating to evidences of indebtedness, the number of shares if relating to shares , and the number of units if relating to any other kind of security . Associate. The term associate, when used to indicate a relationship with any person, means (1) a corporation or organization (other than the registrant or a majority-owned subsidiary of the registrant) of which such person is an officer or partner or is, directly or indirectly, the beneficial owner of 10 percent or more of any class of equity securities, (2) any trust or other estate in which such person has a substantial beneficial interest or as to which such person serves as trustee or in a similar capacity, and (3) any relative or spouse of such person, or any relative of such spouse, who has the same home as such person or who is a director or officer of the registrant or any of its parents or subsidiaries. Automatic shelf registration statement. The term automatic shelf registration statement means a registration statement filed on Form S-3, Form F-3, or Form N-2 ( § 239.13 , § 239.33 , or §§ 239.14 and 274.11a-1 of this chapter) by

perturbation.justification: The modification to the definition of 'Third Party' introduces a legal contradiction by narrowing its scope in a way that conflicts with the SEC's definition of 'Affiliate.' According to 17 CFR § 230.405, an affiliate is 'a person that directly, or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, controls or is controlled by, or is under common control with, the person specified.' The changed text excludes 'an individual investor holding less than 1% equity,' which could include individuals who, despite their small equity stake, exert control or are under common control. This exclusion undermines the standard legal definition of 'affiliate' potentially weakening protections against third-party influence, as explained by the LLM.
